name: Ginza Hachigo
address: 3-14-2 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo (274m from Higashi-Ginza Station, exit A2)
hours: Wed-Sun from 11:00am; selected Tuesdays. Walk-ins 11:00-12:30; reservations 12:30-14:30
reservations: Online via TableCheck. Slots open every Saturday at 9am for the following week, half-hour intervals from 12:30-14:30. 500 yen booking fee/pp. Walk-ins accepted 11:00-12:30 but require arriving early and waiting in line.
whatToOrder: The "Ravioli Gourmandise Chuka Soba" -- features ravioli stuffed with truffle and foie gras atop clear, golden broth made with chicken, clams, dried tomatoes, shiitake, kelp, and prosciutto. Unlike any ramen you have had.
notes: Only 6 counter seats. Was Michelin-starred (Bib Gourmand in 2026 guide). This is a refined, French-influenced ramen experience. The chef previously worked in French cuisine. Not your typical ramen -- this is a destination bowl.
goldenWeek: Likely open (check TableCheck), but slots will book instantly on Saturday mornings.

name: Nakiryu
address: 2-34-4 Minamiotsuka, Toshima-ku, Tokyo (SKY Minamiotsuka 1F, near Otsuka Station south exit)
reservations: None accepted. Walk-in only. Arrive by 10:30am for lunch to minimize wait.
whatToOrder: Tantanmen (dan dan noodle) -- their signature. Rich sesame-chili broth with perfectly balanced heat and nuttiness. The shio ramen is also excellent for something cleaner.
notes: Held a Michelin star 2017-2023, now Bib Gourmand. Only 10 seats. Wait times range from 30 min to 2.5 hours. Go on a weekday and arrive early. This is the best spicy ramen in Tokyo.
goldenWeek: Will be extremely crowded. Consider going May 9 (after GW) instead if you return to Tokyo.

name: Konjiki Hototogisu
address: 2-4-1 Shinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo (Miyaniwa Mansion 1F, room 105, near Shinjuku-Gyoenmae Station)
whatToOrder: Shio soba -- the signature bowl. Broth is an artisanal blend of 60+ ingredients including clam, truffle oil, and porcini. Also try the shoyu soba.
notes: Previously Michelin-starred, now Bib Gourmand. Only 7 counter seats + 2 small tables. Convenient location near Shinjuku Gyoen park. Sophisticated, umami-bomb ramen -- truffle oil adds elegance.

name: Fuunji
address: 2-14-3 Yoyogi, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo (5 min walk from Shinjuku Station south exit)
whatToOrder: Special Tsukemen (dipping noodles). Rich, intense fish-based broth for dipping thick noodles. The broth is viscous and deeply savory -- possibly the best tsukemen in Tokyo.
notes: Near Shinjuku South Exit, NOT near Shibuya hotel (~15-20 min from hotel: JR Yamanote 1 stop to Shinjuku + 5-8 min walk). Backup option for Day 1 dinner (Gyukatsu Motomura is the primary pick — actually walkable from hotel). Perpetual queue but it moves fast. Outstanding for tsukemen specifically. Get the special size (400g) if hungry.

name: Mugi to Olive
address: 6-12-12 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo
whatToOrder: Clam Shio Soba -- elegant, light broth fragrant with clam and olive oil. Feels more like a fine consomme than typical ramen.
notes: Upscale feel, Western-style exterior. Great pairing with Ginza Hachigo if you want a ramen crawl in Ginza.

name: Sushi Yoshikawa
address: 1-6-3 Ebisu, Shibuya City, Tokyo (5 min from Ebisu Station)
reservations: Required. Book via phone or Tabelog.
whatToOrder: 17-piece omakase course. The signature "dragon ball roll" with otoro and uni is the showstopper — one of the most photogenic pieces of sushi in Tokyo. Finished with an unagi hand roll.
notes: Viral on Instagram for the dragon ball roll. At ¥11,000 it's excellent value for the quality and variety. Ebisu is convenient from your Shibuya hotel. A strong contender for your one special sushi meal.

name: Sushi Matsumoto
address: Near Shibuya Station (confirm exact address via Tabelog)
whatToOrder: Lunch Nigiri Set -- 10+ pieces of seasonal nigiri. Excellent quality for the price.
notes: Peaceful atmosphere despite proximity to Shibuya Crossing. Great value lunch option near your hotel.

name: Midori Sushi
address: Multiple locations; Shibuya location on 8F of Shibuya Seibu department store is most convenient for you
hours: Lunch and dinner; closed Jan 1 only
reservations: No reservations. Queue only. Arrive before 11am opening.
whatToOrder: Omakase nigiri set, or order a la carte for the best seasonal fish. The toro is famously generous.
notes: Legendary for quality-to-price ratio. Experienced sushi chefs serving serious sushi at casual prices. Long waits at peak times (30-90 min). The Shibuya branch queue overlooks the crossing. Multiple Tokyo locations.

name: Iki na Sushi Dokoro Abe
address: Roppongi, Tokyo (15 min walk from Roppongi Station)
price: Lunch from 3,000 yen/pp, Dinner from 10,000 yen/pp
whatToOrder: Lunch course or a la carte -- authentic Edomae style at reasonable prices.
notes: From the Ritz-Carlton concierge list. Authentic ambience, experienced itamae (chef).

name: Tsukiji Sushi Omakase
address: 7 min walk from Tsukiji Station
whatToOrder: Their 22-piece omakase -- unusually generous for the price. Menu changes monthly.
notes: Great option to combine with your planned Tsukiji market visit. Over 20 years of chef experience.

name: Sushi SHIN (1 Michelin Star)
address: Nishiazabu, Minato-ku, Tokyo
price: Lunch omakase from ~18,150 yen/pp (~$120). This is your target price point.
reservations: Required. Book via phone (03-5485-0031), WhatsApp (+81-80-6219-9295), email (reservation-tokyo@sushishin.jp), or through the website at sushishin-tokyo.jp/reservations
whatToOrder: Lunch omakase course. Chef Shintaro Suzuki's self-taught Edomae style with personal inspiration. 10+ consecutive years of Michelin stars.
notes: Best value Michelin sushi at lunch in your price range. International clientele means English-friendly. Book well in advance. 72-hour cancellation policy.
goldenWeek: Book NOW for late April dates. Will sell out.

name: Udatsu Sushi (1 Michelin Star)
address: 2-48-10 Kamimeguro, Meguro-ku, Tokyo (5 min walk from Nakameguro Station)
hours: Mon-Fri lunch at 12:00 (single seating); Sat/Sun/holidays at 11:00 and 13:00; Dinner at 18:00 and 21:00
closed: Never, but chef is absent Wed/Sun/holidays (sous-chef serves)
price: Lunch from 14,400 yen/pp (~$95); Dinner from 24,000 yen/pp
reservations: Required. Book via OpenTable or email.
whatToOrder: Omakase lunch -- "Art x Sushi" concept with concrete gallery walls and art pieces. Seasonal ingredients change monthly.
notes: At ~$95 for lunch, this is actually under your budget. Beautiful space, Nakameguro neighborhood is lovely for a walk. Book on a day the head chef is present (Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri/Sat).
goldenWeek: Book immediately for any remaining availability.

name: Udon Maruka
address: 3-16-1 Kanda Ogawamachi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo (New Surugadai Building 1F, Jimbocho area)
reservations: None. Walk-in only. People line up an hour before opening.
whatToOrder: Kamaage udon (served in hot water with dipping sauce) or Bukkake udon. The noodles are Sanuki-style -- thick, impossibly chewy, with a satisfying bite. Considered the gold standard for udon in Tokyo.
notes: Closes when noodles run out (often well before listed closing time). Arrive early. Arguably the single best udon experience in all of Tokyo.
goldenWeek: CLOSED on national holidays (Apr 29, May 3-6). Plan for a weekday visit Apr 25-28 or May 9.

name: Udon Shin
address: Near Shin-Ochanomizu Station, Tokyo (confirm via Google Maps)
reservations: None. Digital queue number system; expect 30+ min wait.
whatToOrder: Curry udon or the tempura udon combo. Handcrafted noodles with perfect chew. The crispy tempura complements the rich broth beautifully.
notes: Another top-tier Tokyo udon spot. The digital queue system lets you explore nearby while waiting.

name: Udon Oniyanma
address: Gotanda Station area, Tokyo
whatToOrder: Cold udon with seasonal tempura. Simple, perfect execution. The chikuwa (fish cake) tempura is legendary.
notes: Standing-style tachigui (stand-and-eat). Ultra-fast, ultra-cheap, ultra-good. Multiple locations.

name: Kisentei
address: Tokyo Midtown, Roppongi (5 min walk from Roppongi Station)
whatToOrder: Lunch course with Ningyocho Imahan Kuroge Wagyu. Private rooms available with counter seating so you watch the chef work.
notes: From the Ritz-Carlton concierge list. Excellent value wagyu teppanyaki lunch. The wagyu is sourced from Imahan, one of Japan's most respected beef purveyors.

name: Steak Hama
address: Roppongi, Tokyo (5 min walk from Roppongi Station)
whatToOrder: Lunch course -- classic teppanyaki since 1964. They do meat, vegetables, and seafood on the griddle.
notes: 60 years of history. Classic, no-nonsense teppanyaki at honest lunch prices. A la carte and course menus available.

name: Ukaitei Omotesando
address: Omotesando, Tokyo
whatToOrder: Lunch course -- all private rooms with dedicated chef cooking on your personal griddle. Modern atmosphere with art throughout.
notes: This is the upscale option. Every table is private with its own chef. If you want one memorable teppanyaki experience, this is it. Also has Ginza and Roppongi branches.

name: Omoide Yokocho (Memory Lane)
address: West side of Shinjuku Station (1 min walk from west exit)
hours: Most stalls open ~17:00-midnight; some open for lunch
closed: Individual stalls vary; most open daily
reservations: None. Squeeze in where you can.
whatToOrder: Everything grilled -- chicken hearts, gizzards, skin, thigh (momo), and negima (chicken and leek). Try motsu (offal) if you are adventurous. Pair with beer or highball.
notes: Dave specifically flagged this as "right by the hotel." Perfect for a casual first-night dinner after arriving from Narita. 70+ tiny stalls. Elbow-to-elbow communal seating. The atmosphere IS the experience.

name: Bird Land (Ginza)
address: 4-2-15 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo (Tsukamoto Sozan Bldg B1F, 103m from Ginza Station exit C6)
reservations: Strongly recommended. Book via OpenTable or phone.
whatToOrder: Omakase course. Chef Toshihiro Wada uses free-range Okukuji Shamo gamecock, grilled over premium Kishu Binchotan charcoal. Every cut -- thigh, breast, liver, heart -- is treated with precision.
notes: This is elite yakitori. One Michelin star. Basement location in Ginza. This is where yakitori becomes art. Counter seats let you watch the master work.
goldenWeek: CLOSED Sundays and public holidays. Plan for a weeknight visit (Apr 25-28 or May 9).

name: Toriki
address: Various locations in Shibuya/Ebisu area
whatToOrder: Salt-grilled chicken parts, especially the tsukune (chicken meatball). Simple, executed perfectly.
notes: Less famous than Bird Land but serious yakitori enthusiasts consider places like Toriki equally excellent. More local vibe, less formality.

name: Ebisu Yokocho
address: Near Ebisu Station (east exit, through the Atre building)
whatToOrder: Hop between stalls. Try the motsu-nabe (offal hot pot), yakitori, and whatever looks good. Order nama beer (draft) or highballs.
notes: This is a network of hidden alleys right near Ebisu Station. Primarily locals -- most tourists do not know about this. Lantern-lit, intimate, authentic. Perfect post-dinner drinks spot.

name: Shinpo
address: Verify via Google Maps; typically in the Kagurazaka or similar local neighborhood
whatToOrder: Creamy crab croquettes, iburigakko (smoked daikon) with cream cheese, seasonal small plates. Pair with Japanese sake or shochu.
notes: Look for the blue noren curtains. This is the kind of place where you sit at the bar, the chef talks to you (in Japanese, mostly), and the food is honest and excellent.

name: Kagurazaka area
address: Kagurazaka neighborhood, near Iidabashi Station
notes: Walking the lantern-lit alleys of Kagurazaka at dusk feels like stepping back in time. Quiet tea houses, charming izakayas. Less tourist-heavy than Shinjuku. Pop into anything that looks inviting with a noren curtain.

name: Tsukiji Outer Market
address: Tsukiji, Chuo-ku, Tokyo (Tsukiji Station or Tsukijishijo Station)
hours: Most stalls 5:00am-14:00. Go in the morning (arrive by 9-10am ideally).
closed: Sundays and some Wednesdays (varies by stall)
payment: Cash at most stalls; some accept cards
notes: The inner wholesale market moved to Toyosu, but the outer market is the real draw for foodies. Over 400 shops and stalls. Go early, go hungry.
goldenWeek: Can be extremely crowded Apr 29-May 6. Your Tokyo dates (Apr 25-28) are pre-Golden Week -- excellent timing for Tsukiji.

name: Ameyoko
address: Under the JR tracks between Ueno and Okachimachi Stations
notes: Less touristy than Tsukiji, more of a local market feel. Good to combine with Ueno area sightseeing. Lively and fun.

name: Yanaka Ginza
address: Near Nippori Station (convenient for your May 9-10 hotel at MyStays Nippori!)
notes: Old-school Tokyo charm. Your May 9-10 hotel is right here -- perfect for a morning stroll on your last day.

name: Tonkatsu Narikura
address: 4-33-9 Naritahigashi, Suginami-ku, Tokyo (3 min walk from Minami-Asagaya Station, Marunouchi Line)
reservations: REQUIRED -- completely reservation-only. Book via Omakase.in or TableCheck. Slots open one week ahead at 12:00 JST. Competition is fierce. Have your hotel concierge help.
whatToOrder: Rosu katsu (pork loin cutlet). No hint of oiliness. The coating is shatteringly crisp, the pork juicy and tender. Tabelog's #1 tonkatsu in Japan.
notes: Worth the effort to book. This will redefine what you think tonkatsu can be. Small, intimate, serious.
goldenWeek: May close during GW. Try for Apr 25-28.

name: Tonkatsu Sugita
address: 3-8-3 Kotobuki, Taito-ku, Tokyo (7 min walk from Tawaramachi Station, 3 min from Kuramae Station exit A5)
hours: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (LO 13:30), Dinner 17:00-21:00 (LO 20:15). Closes when stock runs out.
reservations: Required. Book via Omakase JapanEatinerary, byFood, or SAVOR JAPAN. Phone: 03-3844-5529
whatToOrder: Rosu katsu or hire katsu (tenderloin). Thin, crispy coating -- 10 years of Bib Gourmand recognition. The panko is paper-thin and shatters beautifully.
notes: Near Asakusa -- combine with temple visit. Excellent quality at honest prices.

name: Tonkatsu Hasegawa
address: Ryogoku area, Tokyo
whatToOrder: Their premium Hiraboku Sangenton pork from Yamagata with house-made panko breadcrumbs. Golden, light, never oily.
notes: Newer Bib Gourmand pick. Great if Narikura and Sugita are booked.

name: Shinjuku Kappo Nakajima
address: B1F, Hihara Bldg, 3-32-5 Shinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo (1 min from Shinjuku-sanchome Station exit E9)
whatToOrder: Not curry per se, but sardine set lunches -- Yanagawa Nabe set (990 yen) or any sardine preparation. This is one of Tokyo's cheapest Michelin-starred meals. Complete with rice, miso, pickles, tea.
notes: Listed here because it is an unmissable Michelin lunch deal even though it is sardine-focused, not curry. At under $7 for a Michelin meal, this is borderline criminal value. Queue before 11:30.

name: Akihabara Caligari
address: Kanda area, near Akihabara, Tokyo
whatToOrder: Their Grand Prix-winning curry. Rich, complex, deeply spiced Japanese-style curry.
notes: Won the competitive Kanda Curry Grand Prix. Great to combine with Dave's planned Akihabara electronics trip.

name: Tsuki to Kame
address: Morishita, Tokyo (8 min walk from station)
whatToOrder: Fukagawa Curry Set Meal -- Japanese-South Indian fusion. Only 5 seats. Each dish feels carefully considered.
notes: Deep cut hidden gem. Intimate, meditative dining. Food nerds only.

name: Fileja
address: 2-13-1 Kanda Sudacho, Chiyoda City, Tokyo (Akihabara area)
reservations: Required. Only 8 seats. One chef prepares and serves everything.
whatToOrder: The full omakase — 15 courses with 8 different handmade pasta shapes. Standouts: truffle and crab tagliatelle, lamb ragu gnocchi, braised beef cheek. Every pasta is crafted in front of you.
notes: Blown up on Instagram as one of the best pasta meals in the world. The one-chef, one-kitchen intimacy makes this unforgettable. Not Japanese food per se, but a uniquely Japanese take on Italian mastery. Book ahead.

name: Gyukatsu Motomura
address: Multiple locations: Shibuya (basement level near station), Shinjuku, Harajuku, Akihabara
reservations: Some locations via TableCheck (Shinjuku). Others walk-in only.
whatToOrder: 1.5 cutlet set (195g, 2,600 yen). Breaded beef cutlet arrives rare -- you sear it on a personal hot stone grill at your table. Dip in soy-wasabi, rock salt, or curry sauce.
notes: Uniquely interactive. The beef is tender and the DIY grilling is fun. Shibuya location near your hotel will have 30-60 min waits. Harajuku or Shinjuku may be faster.

name: Shirohige's Cream Puff Factory
address: 5-3-1 Daita, Setagaya-ku, Tokyo (near Shimokitazawa)
whatToOrder: Seasonal Totoro-shaped cream puffs (custard, chocolate, matcha fillings). Also has a cafe upstairs.
notes: Great for Gail and anyone who loves Studio Ghibli. Adorable, photogenic, genuinely delicious.

name: I'm Donut?
address: Multiple locations; Nakameguro is the original
hours: Opens 10:00, sells out by early afternoon
whatToOrder: The original brioche donut with cream filling. Fluffy, light, not too sweet.
notes: Brad loves donuts -- this is a must. Arrive early, they sell out. Also check Higuma Donuts (Hokkaido ingredients) and Mister Donut (Pon de Ring for chewy mochi texture).

name: Pizza Bar on 38th
address: Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, 38th Floor, 2-1-1 Nihonbashi Muromachi, Chuo-ku, Tokyo
reservations: ESSENTIAL. Only 8 seats. Book 2 months ahead via hotel website or phone.
whatToOrder: Omakase pizza course — the chef selects seasonal toppings and creates a progression of pizzas. Neapolitan-style in a wood-fired oven with stunning 38th-floor views.
notes: Ranked #2 on 50 Top Pizza World list. This is a once-in-a-lifetime pizza experience. Counter seating only. Best for a special lunch. Book ASAP for May 9.

name: RistoPizza by Napoli sta ca
address: Azabudai Hills, Minato-ku, Tokyo (near Roppongi/Toranomon)
reservations: Walk-in friendly, especially for lunch. Dinner may have waits.
whatToOrder: Margherita or seasonal specials. Authentic Neapolitan pizza with imported Italian ingredients.
notes: Ranked #13 on 50 Top Pizza World list. Much more accessible than Pizza Bar on 38th — no reservation needed for lunch, reasonable prices. Located in the brand-new Azabudai Hills complex, which is worth exploring on its own.

name: Menya Inoichi
address: Ichijoji area, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto (3 min walk from Ichijoji Station, Eizan Railway)
whatToOrder: Seafood shio ramen -- 100% seafood broth with no animal fat. Delicate, clean, deeply umami. Michelin Bib Gourmand for 9 consecutive years.
notes: Ichijoji is Kyoto's ramen battleground -- a neighborhood with 15+ ramen shops within walking distance of the station. Take the Keihan Railway to Demachiyanagi, transfer to Eizan Railway to Ichijoji (easy). Less touristy than central Kyoto. Make it a mini ramen crawl.

name: Takayasu
address: 3 min walk from Ichijoji Station
whatToOrder: Thick, rich pork broth ramen. The soup simmers for 22 hours. Hearty, filling, and deeply satisfying.
notes: A legend among Kyoto University students since 1999. Student-friendly prices, serious quality. Great contrast to Inoichi's delicate seafood style.

name: Wagyu Ramen Julia
address: 7 min walk from Gion-Shijo Station, Kyoto
whatToOrder: Wagyu beef broth ramen -- luxurious, slow-simmered beef broth with deep umami. They may give you a bonus wagyu slice on weekday afternoons.
notes: Hidden gem in Gion. Uncrowded despite location. Walking distance from your hotel.

name: Kiramekinotori
address: Central Kyoto (check exact location)
whatToOrder: Thick chicken broth (tori paitan) ramen. Rich, creamy, comforting.
notes: Open late, open every day -- excellent fallback option. From the Ritz-Carlton concierge guide.

name: Sushidokoro Man
address: Central Kyoto (check Tabelog for exact address, near Shijo area)
whatToOrder: Omakase or a la carte. Both available. Quality Edomae-style sushi at reasonable Kyoto prices.
notes: From the Ritz-Carlton list -- solid mid-range option.

name: Hiyoko Sushi
address: Confirm via Tabelog
whatToOrder: 10-piece omakase set. Under a dollar per piece with far fresher ingredients than chain sushi.
notes: Best bang-for-your-buck sushi in Kyoto. Locals' favorite.

name: Sushi Kawano
address: Near Kamo River, Kyoto
whatToOrder: Edomae-style omakase with careful aging technique. Exquisite umami from the aging process.
notes: Intimate 9-seat counter. High-end technique at affordable prices.

name: Taisushi
address: Confirm via Tabelog
whatToOrder: Omakase nigiri -- simple, retro atmosphere. Locals love it.
notes: Walk-in friendly, no fuss. The kind of local sushi bar where regulars sit at the counter.

name: Gion Takuma
address: Shirakawa-dori street, near Gionshijo Station (by the Shirakawa river)
price: Lunch kaiseki from 3,600 yen/pp (~$24) -- exceptional value
whatToOrder: Lunch kaiseki course -- includes otsukuri-no-moriawase (sashimi platter) and seasonal specialties. Beautiful presentation at an absurdly low price for this quality level.
notes: Walking distance from your hotel. This is your best budget kaiseki option. The location along the Shirakawa river is gorgeous.

name: Pontocho Fujita
address: Pontocho, Kyoto (walking distance from your hotel)
whatToOrder: Lunch set menu. Pontocho alley location adds atmosphere.
notes: Pontocho is a narrow, atmospheric alleyway along the Kamo River. Some restaurants here have river-facing terraces (kawadoko) in spring/summer.

name: Saiseki Chimoto
address: Central Kyoto
whatToOrder: Lunch set menu -- described as "casual kaiseki," meaning less formal but same attention to seasonal ingredients and presentation.

name: Rengetsu-jaya
address: Higashiyama district, Kyoto
whatToOrder: Tofu kaiseki -- yudofu (hot tofu), yuba (tofu skin), namafu (wheat gluten). Served in a traditional machiya townhouse.
notes: Kyoto is the tofu capital of Japan. This is an authentic way to experience a uniquely Kyoto cuisine. Seasonal menus change regularly.

name: Tenyu
address: Central Kyoto
price: Lunch tempura rice bowl or set from ~3,000-5,000 yen/pp
whatToOrder: Tempura rice bowl (tendon) at lunch -- freshest ingredients with light, crispy batter.

name: Kitenya
address: Central Kyoto, near Shijo Station
whatToOrder: Tempura course or set meal. Everything arrives moments after leaving the oil -- the freshness is the star. Light batter, seasonal vegetables, shrimp.
notes: Bib Gourmand recognition for "good quality cooking that is also good value." Central location makes it easy to work into any day.

name: Yoshikawa Tempura
address: Near the Imperial Palace, Kyoto (housed in a machiya with garden)
whatToOrder: Counter-seat tempura course -- sit at the counter and watch the chef work. The setting (traditional machiya with koi pond and bonsai garden) is half the experience.
notes: More upscale option. Beautiful traditional building. Counter seats are the best experience.

name: Omen
address: 171-1 Kashiwayacho, Shijo-Dori Ponto-cho Nishiiru, Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto
whatToOrder: Omen udon -- thick udon noodles with hot or cold dipping broth, served with seasonal vegetables on the side. Vegetarian option available.
notes: From the Ritz-Carlton list. One of Kyoto's most beloved udon restaurants. The Pontocho location is walking distance from your hotel. The Ginkakuji (Silver Pavilion) location is the original if you are in that area.

name: Honke Owariya
address: 322 Niomon-Tsuchikutsukucho, Nijo-sagaru, Kurumayacho-Dori, Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto
whatToOrder: Horai Soba -- a multi-tiered serving of cold soba with an array of toppings. Five layers, each slightly different. It is ceremonial, beautiful, and delicious.
notes: Founded in 1465. That is not a typo -- 550+ years old. The atmospheric old wooden building is worth the visit alone. One of Kyoto's most legendary dining experiences. Simple food, perfect execution, centuries of tradition.

name: Roujina
address: Central Kyoto
whatToOrder: Soba -- handmade buckwheat noodles with clean, nutty flavor.

name: Nishiki Market
address: 5-block stretch between Shijo-dori and Nishikikoji-dori, Nakagyo-ku (walking distance from your hotel!)
closed: Some stalls closed Wednesdays; most open during Golden Week
notes: 120+ shops across 5 blocks. Arrive 10-11am to beat crowds. Your hotel is within walking distance. Plan 1.5-2 hours minimum.
goldenWeek: Will be extremely crowded. Go first thing in the morning.

name: Yudofu
notes: Kyoto's silken tofu is unlike anything you have had. The water in Kyoto produces exceptionally smooth, delicate tofu.

name: Saba-zushi
notes: Kyoto's signature sushi -- vinegared mackerel pressed over rice. Developed as a preservation method when fish had to travel from the Sea of Japan to landlocked Kyoto. A must-try.

name: Kyogoku Kaneyo
address: Central Kyoto
whatToOrder: Kinshi-don -- eel over rice topped with a thin egg omelet (the house signature). Rich, sweet-savory glazed eel.
notes: Unagi is a quintessential Japanese experience. This spot is recommended by the Ritz-Carlton concierge.

name: Hikiniku to Come
address: Near Kawaramachi, Kyoto (walking distance from Hotel Forza)
hours: Lunch and dinner, check current schedule
reservations: Walk-in only, expect a queue (especially Golden Week)
whatToOrder: The signature charcoal-grilled hamburger patty with rice and miso soup. Hand-chopped beef grilled over binchotan charcoal. Simple, perfect.
notes: Cult-favorite Kyoto spot that's blown up on Instagram. The patties are juicy and smoky — nothing like a Western hamburger. Served with rice, miso soup, and pickles. Worth the wait. Best for lunch or early dinner to beat the queue.

name: Dotonbori
address: Dotonbori-dori and surrounding streets, Chuo-ku, Osaka (walking distance from your hotel)
payment: Mix of cash and cards; bring cash
notes: This is the beating heart of Osaka food culture. Neon-lit, chaotic, delicious. Your hotel is right here. Go multiple times -- once at night for the atmosphere, once during the day to actually taste things carefully.
goldenWeek: May 4-6 will be PACKED. Your birthday dinner May 6 reservation is smart -- walk-in dining will have long waits.

name: Shinsekai
address: Shinsekai, Naniwa-ku, Osaka (about 15 min subway from your hotel)
notes: Feels like stepping back 50 years. This is old-school Osaka. The Tsutenkaku Tower is here if you want a landmark. Less touristy than Dotonbori, more authentically gritty.

name: Umaiya
address: 4-21 Naniwacho, Kita Ward, Osaka
whatToOrder: Their unconventional style -- no bonito flakes, no nori, no green onions, no mayo. Just crispy tenkasu and pickled ginger inside the batter. Pure takoyaki essence.
notes: This is different from every other takoyaki you will find. Locals consider it among the best. Stripping away the toppings lets you taste the batter and octopus.

name: Takotsubo
address: Confirm via Tabelog (not in tourist center)
whatToOrder: Classic Osaka-style takoyaki. Crispy exterior, molten center.
notes: Listed on the Michelin guide five times. Away from tourist crowds -- this is where locals go.

name: Oko Takoyaki
address: Along Shinsaibashi, Osaka
whatToOrder: Creative variations on classic takoyaki. Locals call this the best in Osaka.
notes: Small restaurant filled with authenticity. Near your hotel in Shinsaibashi.

name: Sanchan-Ya
address: Near Nakatsu Station (1 stop from Umeda on Midosuji Line)
whatToOrder: Generous chunks of octopus, perfectly crispy exterior. Authentic local hole-in-the-wall.
notes: One subway stop from Umeda. Worth the detour for serious takoyaki.

name: Kiji (Michelin Bib Gourmand)
address: Shin-Umeda Shokudogai, 9-20 Kakuda-cho, Kita-ku, Osaka (also a branch in Umeda Sky Building basement)
whatToOrder: Butatama (pork and egg) or the modanyaki (with noodles). The secret is the ooba (perilla leaf) mixed in -- gives it a unique, zingy freshness.
notes: Michelin Bib Gourmand for good reason. 50+ years in business. Located in Shin-Umeda Shokudo-gai, a bustling underground dining street with ~100 establishments. Expect 30-45 min wait at peak hours.
goldenWeek: Closed Sundays. Go on a weekday.

name: Mizuno
address: Dotonbori, Osaka (walking distance from your hotel)
reservations: Check if accepted; often walk-in with queue
whatToOrder: Yamaimo-yaki (mountain yam base makes it incredibly fluffy) or the seafood deluxe. Featured on Netflix.
notes: Open since 1945, third generation. One of the most famous okonomiyaki restaurants in Japan. The mountain yam batter makes their version noticeably lighter and fluffier than competitors.

name: Ajinoya
address: Namba area, Osaka
whatToOrder: Traditional okonomiyaki with decades of refined technique.
notes: Bib Gourmand certified. Very authentic vibe, frequented by locals. Less tourist-heavy than Mizuno.

name: Hozenji-yokocho Yakizen
address: Hozenji-yokocho (narrow cobblestone alley near Dotonbori Canal)
whatToOrder: Traditional okonomiyaki served over the bar. The setting in the atmospheric alley is magical.
notes: Hozenji-yokocho is one of Osaka's most charming alleys -- stone-paved, lantern-lit, with a moss-covered Buddhist statue. Your birthday dinner restaurant (Matsuzaka Gyu Yakiniku M Hozenji-Hanare) is also in this alley! Combine an okonomiyaki lunch here with scouting the area before your birthday dinner.

name: Matsuzaka Gyu Yakiniku M Hozenji-Hanare
address: Hozenji-yokocho, Osaka
notes: Already confirmed. Happy 40th birthday, Brad!

name: Shinsaibashi Yakiniku En
address: Near Shinsaibashi Station, Osaka (walking distance from hotel)
whatToOrder: Premium Kuroge Wagyu selections. They offer A5 beef at prices that feel too good.
notes: Private rooms available. Relaxed atmosphere. Good option for a casual yakiniku night separate from your birthday dinner.

address: Multiple Osaka locations
whatToOrder: Their 1,980 yen course with short rib, tongue, and harami (skirt steak) is the sweet spot.
notes: Unbelievable value. Not premium wagyu, but solid quality at an absurd price. Good for a casual group meal if you want yakiniku without the splurge. Also has a 1,480 yen course featuring beef tongue.

name: Yaekatsu
address: Shinsekai, Osaka
whatToOrder: Assorted kushikatsu -- meat, seafood, vegetables. The batter here has been perfected over 75+ years.
notes: Hidden gem locals love. Staff will help even though menu is in Japanese. Way better than Daruma for actual food quality.

name: Kushikatsu Kei
address: 5 min walk from Midoribashi Station, Osaka Metro Chuo Line
whatToOrder: Vegetable kushikatsu is surprisingly excellent here. Their house sauce is mixed with chicken broth -- the "phantom sauce."
notes: Beautiful setting in a renovated private house with exposed historical beams. Elevated kushikatsu experience. Not a tourist spot at all.

name: Shichifukujin
address: Tenma, Osaka
whatToOrder: Kushikatsu + oden (simmered dishes) + cheap draft beer. Counter-seat only.
notes: Super popular with locals. The oden is a great palate cleanser between fried skewers. Tenma is a fantastic local neighborhood for bar hopping.

name: Kushikatsu Daruma
address: Multiple Dotonbori locations (the angry-faced sign is unmistakable)
whatToOrder: Assorted set. Classic Osaka-style kushikatsu.
notes: Yes, it is touristy. Yes, the food is still genuinely good. Worth trying once for the atmosphere, but the spots above are where locals actually eat. RULE: never double-dip in the communal sauce.

name: Shoyu-Ramen Sodaisho
address: Temma, Osaka
whatToOrder: Shoyu chicken-clam ramen. Baseball-themed decor. Light, flavorful, satisfying.
notes: Long-running local favorite in Temma. Removed from the Dotonbori tourist grind. Authentically local experience.

name: Chukasoba Kazura
address: Awaza, Osaka
whatToOrder: Shoyu chicken ramen with a "mousse-like" broth -- fabulously light and airy. A cut above the rest.
notes: Queue with the locals. Worth it.

name: Hifumi
address: Koubai-cho, Osaka
whatToOrder: Rich tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen -- authentic Hakata style transplanted to Osaka. Creamy, porky, with thin firm noodles.
notes: If you are craving that thick Hakata-style tonkotsu, this is your spot.

name: Harukoma (Tenjinbashi)
address: 5-5-2 Tenjinbashi, Kita Ward, Osaka (in the Tenjinbashi shopping arcade, near Tenjinbashisuji-Rokuchome Station)
reservations: Recommended for peak times; walk-in with potential 30-60 min wait
whatToOrder: A la carte -- toro, uni, anago (sea eel), ikura. The portions are famously generous. 3 people ate until full for 4,000 yen total.
notes: Locals' favorite sushi in Osaka. Insane value -- quality fish at prices that seem impossible. Located in Japan's longest shopping arcade (Tenjinbashisuji). Arrive early to minimize wait.

name: Sushi Kappou Mori
address: Confirm via Tabelog
price: Under 5,000 yen for 12-piece nigiri course with 3 small plates and soup
whatToOrder: Full course -- 12 pieces of nigiri, three small plates, and soup. Locals say "gourmet quality at half price."
notes: Outstanding value. Less well-known than Harukoma, which means shorter waits.

name: Echizen Sushi
address: Shinsaibashi, Osaka
whatToOrder: Lunch set -- cheap, delicious, loved by locals. Walking distance from Cross Hotel.
notes: Convenient location near your hotel for a quick, quality sushi lunch.
